sourceAnswer: C. {ww^R | w in {a, b}*}

parsed.stem: Which of the following languages over {a, b, c} is accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton?

options.text: {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 0}
{ww | w in {a, b}*}
{ww^R | w in {a, b}*}
{w | w is a palindrome over {a, b, c}} Note: w^R is the string obtained by reversing w.

sourceQuestion:
Let L_F be the set of all languages accepted by a PDA by final state and L_e be the set of all languages accepted by empty stack. Which of the following is true?
A. L_F = L_e
B. L_F subset L_e
C. L_e subset L_F
D. None
sourceAnswer: A. L_F = L_e

parsed.stem: Let L_F be the set of all languages accepted by a PDA by final state and L_e be the set of all languages accepted by empty stack. Which of the following is true?

options.text: L_F = L_e
L_F subset L_e
L_e subset L_F
None

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following is FALSE?

A. There is a unique minimal DFA for every regular language.
B. Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent PDA.
C. Complement of every context-free language is recursive.
D. Every nondeterministic PDA can be converted to an equivalent deterministic PDA.
sourceAnswer: D. Every nondeterministic PDA can be converted to an equivalent deterministic PDA.

parsed.stem: Which one of the following is FALSE?

options.text: There is a unique minimal DFA for every regular language.
Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent PDA.
Complement of every context-free language is recursive.
Every nondeterministic PDA can be converted to an equivalent deterministic PDA.

parsed.stem:
Consider the NPDA Q = ({q0, q1}, {0,1}, {0,1,A,q0}, delta, q0, A, F = {q0}),
where (as per usual convention) Q is the set of states, Sigma is the input alphabet, Gamma is the stack alphabet, delta is the state transition function, q0 is the initial state, A is the initial stack symbol, and F is the set of accepting states.

The state transition is as follows:
delta(q0, 0,

options.text: = {(q1, A)} delta(q0, 0, 0) = {(q1, 0)} delta(q1, 0, A) = {(q0, 00A)} delta(q1, 0, 0) = {(q0, 000)} delta(q0, 1, A) = {(q0, A)} delta(q0, 1, 0) = {(q0, epsilon)} Which one of the following sequences is accepted by the automaton? A. 10110

sourceQuestion:
Consider the transition diagram of a PDA given below with input alphabet Sigma = {a, b} and stack alphabet = {X, Z}. Z is the initial stack symbol. Let L denote the language accepted by the PDA.

The PDA has states q0, q1, q2 with transitions:
- q0 -> q0: a, Z/XZ (push X on Z)
- q0 -> q0: a, X/XX (push X on X)
- q0 -> q1: epsilon, Z/Z (epsilon transition)
- q0 -> q1: epsilon, X/X (epsilon transition)
- q1 -> q1: b, X/epsilon (pop X on reading b)
- q1 -> q2: epsilon, Z/Z
- q2 is the accepting state

Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. L = {a^n b^n | n >= 0} and L is not accepted by any finite automata
B. L = {a^n | n >= 0} U {a^n b^n | n >= 0} and L is not accepted by any deterministic PDA
C. L is not accepted by any Turing machine that halts on every input
D. L = {a^n | n >= 0} U {a^n b^n | n >= 0} and L is deterministic context-free
sourceAnswer: D. L = {a^n | n >= 0} U {a^n b^n | n >= 0} and L is deterministic context-free

parsed.stem:
Consider the transition diagram of a PDA given below with input alphabet Sigma = {a, b} and stack alphabet = {X, Z}. Z is the initial stack symbol. Let L denote the language accepted by the PDA.

The PDA has states q0, q1, q2 with transitions:
- q0 -> q0: a, Z/XZ (push X on Z)
- q0 -> q0: a, X/XX (push X on X)
- q0 -> q1: epsilon, Z/Z (epsilon transition)
- q0 -> q1: epsilon, X/X (epsilon transition)
- q1 -> q1: b, X/epsilon (pop X on reading b)
- q1 -> q2: epsilon, Z/Z
- q2 is the accepting state

Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: L = {a^n b^n | n >= 0} and L is not accepted by any finite automata
L = {a^n | n >= 0} U {a^n b^n | n >= 0} and L is not accepted by any deterministic PDA
L is not accepted by any Turing machine that halts on every input
L = {a^n | n >= 0} U {a^n b^n | n >= 0} and L is deterministic context-free

parsed.stem:
Consider the pushdown automaton (PDA) P below, which runs on the input alphabet {a, b}, has stack alphabet {Z, A}, and has three states {q, p, f}, with q being the start state. A transition from state to state s, labelled c/X -> y, where c is an input symbol or epsilon, X is a stack symbol, and y is a string of stack symbols, represents the fact that in state s the PDA can read c from the input, pop X off the top of the stack, push y (in order) on the stack, and go to state t.

In the initial configuration, the stack has only the symbol Z at the bottom. The PDA accepts by empty stack.

The PDA has transitions:
- From q: a/Z -> AZ goes to q, a/A -> AA goes to q, b/A -> epsilon goes to p
- From p: b/A -> epsilon goes to p, epsilon/Z -> epsilon goes to f

Which one of the following options correctly describes the language accepted by P?

options.text: {a^m b^n | 1 <= m and n <= m}
{a^m | 0 <= m} union {a^m b^n | 0 <= n}

sourceQuestion:
Let M = (K, Sigma, Gamma, delta, s, F) be a pushdown automaton, where K = {q1, q2}, Sigma = {a}, Gamma = {b}, s = q1, F = {q2}, and delta is given by:
  delta(q1, a, epsilon) = {(q1, b)}
  delta(q1, a, b) = {(q2, epsilon)}
  delta(q2, a, b) = {(q2, epsilon)}

Which one of the following strings is NOT a member of L(M)?

A. aab
B. aabb (but interpreted as input string 'aab' with acceptance)
C. aaab
D. aaaabb

(Note: From the image, the actual options are strings over {a}. The options are:
A. a (single a)
B. aaa
C. aaaa
D. aaaaa)
sourceAnswer: D. aaaaa

options.text: aab
aabb (but interpreted as input string 'aab' with acceptance)
aaab
aaaabb (Note: From the image, the actual options are strings over {a}. The options are: A. a (single a) B. aaa C. aaaa D. aaaaa)

sourceAnswer: B. N(M) is necessarily Sigma* but L(M) is not necessarily Sigma*

options.text: L(M) is necessarily Sigma* but N(M) is not necessarily Sigma*
N(M) is necessarily Sigma* but L(M) is not necessarily Sigma*
Both L(M) and N(M) are necessarily Sigma*
Neither L(M) nor N(M) are necessarily Sigma*

sourceAnswer: B. {ww^R | w in {a, b}*}

parsed.stem: Which of the following languages is accepted by a non-deterministic pushdown automaton (PDA) but NOT by a deterministic PDA?

options.text: {ww^R | w in {a, b}*}

sourceAnswer: B. {a^l b^m | l >= m, m > 0}

parsed.stem:
Consider the pushdown automaton (PDA) below which runs over the input alphabet {a, b}. It has the stack alphabet {Z_0, X} where Z_0 is the bottom-of-stack marker. The set of states of the PDA is {q_0, q_1, q_f} where q_0 is the start state and q_f is the final state. The PDA accepts by final state. The transitions of the PDA are:
(q_0, a, Z_0) -> (q_0, XZ_0)
(q_0, a, X) -> (q_0, XX)
(q_0, b, X) -> (q_1, epsilon)
(q_1, b, X) -> (q_1, epsilon)
(q_1, epsilon, Z_0) -> (q_f, epsilon)

The language accepted by the PDA is:

options.text: {a^l b^m | l = m}
{a^l b^m | l >= m, m > 0}
{a^l b^m | l = 2m}
{a^l b^m | l > m, m > 0}

sourceQuestion:
Recursive languages are:
A. A proper superset of context free languages.
B. Always recognizable by pushdown automata.
C. Also called type 0 languages.
D. Recognizable by Turing machines that always halt.
sourceAnswer: A, D

parsed.stem: Recursive languages are:

options.text: A proper superset of context free languages.
Always recognizable by pushdown automata.
Also called type 0 languages.
Recognizable by Turing machines that always halt.

sourceQuestion:
Nobody knows yet if P = NP. Consider the language L defined as:
L = { (0+1)* if P = NP, and {0} otherwise }
Which of the following statements is true?
A. L is recursive
B. L is recursively enumerable but not recursive
C. L is not recursively enumerable
D. Whether L is recursively enumerable or not will be known after we find out if P = NP
sourceAnswer: A. L is recursive

options.text: L is recursive
L is recursively enumerable but not recursive
L is not recursively enumerable
Whether L is recursively enumerable or not will be known after we find out if P = NP

sourceQuestion:
If the strings of a language L can be effectively enumerated in lexicographic (i.e., alphabetic) order, which of the following statements is true?
A. L is necessarily finite
B. L is regular but not necessarily finite
C. L is context free but not necessarily regular
D. L is recursive but not necessarily context free
sourceAnswer: D. L is recursive but not necessarily context free

parsed.stem: If the strings of a language L can be effectively enumerated in lexicographic (i.e., alphabetic) order, which of the following statements is true?

options.text: L is necessarily finite
L is regular but not necessarily finite
L is context free but not necessarily regular
L is recursive but not necessarily context free

sourceQuestion:
Define languages L_0 and L_1 as follows:
L_0 = {(M, w, 0) | M halts on w}
L_1 = {(M, w, 1) | M does not halt on w}
Here (M, w, c) is a triplet whose first component is an encoding of a Turing Machine, second component is a string, and third component is a bit.
Let L = L_0 union L_1. Which of the following is true?
A. L_0 is recursively enumerable, but L_1 is not
B. L_1 is recursively enumerable, but L_0 is not
C. Both L_0 and L_1 are recursive
D. Neither L nor L_1 is recursively enumerable
sourceAnswer: A. L_0 is recursively enumerable, but L_1 is not

options.text: L_0 is recursively enumerable, but L_1 is not
L_1 is recursively enumerable, but L_0 is not
Both L_0 and L_1 are recursive
Neither L nor L_1 is recursively enumerable

sourceQuestion:
L_1 is a recursively enumerable language over Sigma. An algorithm A effectively enumerates its words as w_1, w_2, w_3, ... . Here # is a new symbol. Define another language over Sigma union {#} as:
L_2 = {w_i # w_j | i < j}
Consider the following assertions:
S_1: L_2 is recursive implies L_1 is recursive
S_2: L_1 is recursive implies L_2 is recursive
Which of the following statements is true?
A. Both S_1 and S_2 are true
B. S_1 is true but S_2 is not necessarily true
C. S_2 is true but S_1 is not necessarily true
D. Neither S_1 nor S_2 is necessarily true
sourceAnswer: A. Both S_1 and S_2 are true

parsed.stem:

options.text: Both S_1 and S_2 are true
S_1 is true but S_2 is not necessarily true
S_2 is true but S_1 is not necessarily true
Neither S_1 nor S_2 is necessarily true

sourceQuestion:
Let L_1 be a recursive language, and let L_2 be a recursively enumerable but not a recursive language. Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. L_1' is recursive and L_2' is recursively enumerable
B. L_1' is recursive and L_2' is not recursively enumerable
C. L_1' and L_2' are recursively enumerable
D. L_1' is recursively enumerable and L_2' is recursive
sourceAnswer: B. L_1' is recursive and L_2' is not recursively enumerable

parsed.stem: Let L_1 be a recursive language, and let L_2 be a recursively enumerable but not a recursive language. Which one of the following is TRUE?

L_1' and L_2' are recursively enumerable
L_1' is recursively enumerable and L_2' is recursive

sourceQuestion:
If L_1 and L_2 are recursively enumerable then L_1 intersection L_2 is
A. regular
B. context-free
C. context-sensitive
D. recursive
sourceAnswer: C. context-sensitive

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is FALSE?
A. Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA
B. Every non-deterministic Turing machine can be converted to an equivalent deterministic Turing machine
C. Every regular language is also a context-free language
D. Every subset of a recursively enumerable language is recursively enumerable
sourceAnswer: D. Every subset of a recursively enumerable language is recursively enumerable

sourceQuestion:
Let L be a recursive language. Let L_1 and L_2 be languages that are recursively enumerable but not recursive. Which of the following statements is not necessarily true?
I. Complement of L_1 is recursively enumerable
II. L \ L_1 is recursively enumerable
III. L_1 intersection L_2 is recursively enumerable
IV. L_1 union L_2 is recursively enumerable

A. I only
B. III only
C. III and IV only
D. I and IV only
sourceAnswer: A. I only

sourceQuestion:
Let L be a language and L_bar be its complement. Which one of the following is NOT a viable possibility?
A. Neither L nor L_bar is recursively enumerable.
B. One of L and L_bar is r.e. but not recursive; the other is not r.e.
C. Both L and L_bar are r.e. but not recursive.
D. Both L and L_bar are recursive.
sourceAnswer: C. Both L and L_bar are r.e. but not recursive.

Both L and L_bar are r.e. but not recursive.

sourceQuestion:
Let A <=_m B denotes that language A is mapping reducible (also known as many-to-one reducible) to language B. Which one of the following is FALSE?
A. If A <=_m B and B is recursive then A is recursive.
B. If A <=_m B and A is undecidable then B is undecidable.
C. If A <=_m B and B is recursively enumerable then A is recursively enumerable.
D. If A <=_m B and A is not recursively enumerable then B is not recursively enumerable.
sourceAnswer: D. If A <=_m B and A is not recursively enumerable then B is not recursively enumerable.

parsed.stem: Let A <=_m B denotes that language A is mapping reducible (also known as many-to-one reducible) to language B. Which one of the following is FALSE?

options.text: If A <=_m B and B is recursive then A is recursive.
If A <=_m B and A is undecidable then B is undecidable.
If A <=_m B and B is recursively enumerable then A is recursively enumerable.
If A <=_m B and A is not recursively enumerable then B is not recursively enumerable.

sourceQuestion:
Let <M> be the encoding of a Turing machine as a string over {0, 1}. Let L = {<M> | M is a Turing machine that accepts a string of length 2014}. Then L is:

A. decidable and recursively enumerable
B. undecidable but recursively enumerable
C. undecidable and not recursively enumerable
D. decidable but not recursively enumerable
sourceAnswer: B. undecidable but recursively enumerable

options.text: decidable and recursively enumerable
undecidable but recursively enumerable
undecidable and not recursively enumerable
decidable but not recursively enumerable

sourceQuestion:
For any two languages L1 and L2 such that L1 is context-free and L2 is recursively enumerable but not recursive, which of the following is/are necessarily true?

I. L1' (Complement of L1) is recursive
II. L2' (Complement of L2) is recursive
III. L1 intersection L2 is context-free
IV. L1 union L2 is recursively enumerable

A. I only
B. III only
C. III and IV only
D. I and IV only
sourceAnswer: D. I and IV only

sourceQuestion:
For a Turing machine M, <M> denotes an encoding of M. Consider the following two languages:
L_1 = {<M> | M is a DFA such that L(M) = Sigma*}
L_2 = {<M> | M takes more than 2021 steps on some input}

Which one of the following options is correct?
A. Both L_1 and L_2 are decidable
B. L_1 is decidable and L_2 is undecidable
C. L_1 is undecidable and L_2 is decidable
D. Both L_1 and L_2 are undecidable
sourceAnswer: B. L_1 is decidable and L_2 is undecidable

options.text: Both L_1 and L_2 are decidable
L_1 is decidable and L_2 is undecidable
L_1 is undecidable and L_2 is decidable
Both L_1 and L_2 are undecidable

sourceQuestion:
Consider three decision problems P_1, P_2 and P_3. It is known that P_1 is decidable and P_2 is undecidable. Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. P_3 is decidable if P_1 is reducible to P_3
B. P_3 is undecidable if P_3 is reducible to P_2
C. P_3 is undecidable if P_2 is reducible to P_3
D. P_3 is decidable if P_3 is reducible to P_2's complement
sourceAnswer: C. P_3 is undecidable if P_2 is reducible to P_3

parsed.stem: Consider three decision problems P_1, P_2 and P_3. It is known that P_1 is decidable and P_2 is undecidable. Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: P_3 is decidable if P_1 is reducible to P_3
P_3 is undecidable if P_3 is reducible to P_2
P_3 is undecidable if P_2 is reducible to P_3
P_3 is decidable if P_3 is reducible to P_2's complement

sourceQuestion:
Let X be a recursive language and Y be a recursively enumerable but not recursive language. Let W and Z be two languages such that Y reduces to W, and Z reduces to X (reduction means the standard many-one reduction). Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
A. W can be recursively enumerable and Z is recursive
B. W can be recursively enumerable and Z is recursively enumerable
C. Z is recursively enumerable and W is recursive
D. W is not recursively enumerable and Z is recursive
sourceAnswer: A. W can be recursively enumerable and Z is recursive

parsed.stem: Let X be a recursive language and Y be a recursively enumerable but not recursive language. Let W and Z be two languages such that Y reduces to W, and Z reduces to X (reduction means the standard many-one reduction). Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: W can be recursively enumerable and Z is recursive
W can be recursively enumerable and Z is recursively enumerable
Z is recursively enumerable and W is recursive
W is not recursively enumerable and Z is recursive

sourceQuestion:
Let r = 1(1 + 0)*, s = 1*0 and t = 1*0 be three regular expressions. Which one of the following is true?
A. L(s) subset L(r) and L(s) subset L(t)
B. L(r) subset L(s) and L(s) subset L(t)
C. L(t) subset L(r) and L(s) subset L(t)
D. L(s) subset L(r) and L(t) subset L(r)
E. None of the above
sourceAnswer: D

parsed.stem: Let r = 1(1 + 0)*, s = 1*0 and t = 1*0 be three regular expressions. Which one of the following is true?

L(t) subset L(r) and L(s) subset L(t)

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following regular expression identities is/are TRUE?
A. r^* = (r + epsilon)^*
B. r^* = r^* + epsilon
C. (r + s)^* = r^* + s^*
D. r^* s^* = (r^* + s^*)^*
sourceAnswer: A, B
